metadata.title: Translating the norm bundle of an international regime: states’ pledges on climate change around the 2015 Paris conference
metadata.journal: Journal of International Relations and Development

abstract: International regimes contain norm bundles with several distinct norms that jointly define what is expected from parties. As states engage with these regime-inherent norms, they attribute meanings to them. Moreover, they can also translate norms extraneous to the regime. Here we take a theoretical middle ground between full-fledged norm adoption and approaches that emphasize the novelty of each translation. We argue that norm translations may be largely shared among states with similar domestic contexts. This includes individual translations of norms as well as prioritizations between them. Empirically, we focus on the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) by applying unsupervised quantitative text analysis (topic modeling) to the Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) submitted around the 2015 Paris conference. Studying these implementation pledges, we find that states have mostly engaged in regime-inherent norm translations, indicating normative stability of the regime. This occurred in distinct forms, such as by translating mitigation responsibilities in the form of absolute or relative emission reduction targets. We find hints that norm translations indeed vary in line with states' domestic context factors: Beyond broad differences between industrialized and developing countries, we show the relevance of factors like vulnerability to climate change and emissions levels.

paragraphs: The 1992 United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) touches on a wide range of normative provisions on how parties should contribute to preventing dangerous climate change. Among others, they cover differentiated mitigation responsibilities, adaptation to climate change, and even the promotion of economic development. In our view, the UNFCCC thus contains a norm bundle with various regime-inherent norms that reflect a range of expectations about how parties should achieve the objective of the international climate regime. In the context of the 2015 Conference of the Parties (COP) in Paris, states were then invited to submit their Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs). These documents outline how each party intends to contribute to reaching the objective of the UNFCCC, providing us with an opportunity to study norm translations in the context of implementation pledges made by state parties. Loose and nonbinding guidelines for drafting these documents were adopted at the 2014 conference in Lima. However, both the original Convention and the Lima guidelines are negotiated compromises. So it can be assumed that the NDCs will include varying meaning attributions to the UNFCCC's norms. As these documents were prepared in a voluntary bottom-up fashion with little guidance from the UNFCCC, we could even expect states to align their climate-related implementation pledges with normative perspectives from outside the regime. Due to their hybrid position between the national and the international level, the NDCs are particularly useful to answer our research question: How do states translate norms in their implementation pledges for achieving the objective of an international regime? Some scholars have analyzed the workings of a collection of norms institutionalized in an issue area, while treating each of them as a stable individual norm that is either adopted or rejected (e.g., Fukuda-Parr and Hulme 2011: 18; Bloomfield and Scott 2017: 3; Lantis and Wunderlich 2018). In contrast, norm translation research emphasizes that individual norms change their meaning when actors engage with them, and that these translations are based on actors' background (e.g., Berger 2017). We point to a theoretical middle ground between approaches focusing on full-fledged norm adoption and those that emphasize the novelty of each norm translation. Our concept of norm bundle translations contributes to these literatures in two ways. First, our concept captures the distinct, but largely shared meaning attributions by states regarding individual norms, and distinguishes between regime-inherent (i.e. concerning the norm bundle) and regimeextraneous norm translations. Second, agents subject to the norm bundle can prioritize some of these norms over others. This norm prioritization serves to assess how states translate the norm bundle as a whole. Both individual translations and prioritization can be traced back to states' domestic context factors.

We introduce a methodological innovation to the norm translation literature by conducting a quantitative content analysis (see Grimmer and Stewart 2013 for an overview). In contrast to the predominant qualitative case study research to investigate meaning attributions to single norms, we reveal norm translations of all states that submitted an NDC to the UNFCCC. Topic modeling is an inductive and exploratory method: patterns in the texts are identified based on the joint cooccurrence of words, representing (thematic) topics. Crucially, the method does not make prior assumptions about which topics feature in the text corpus. Only in a second, qualitative step of the analysis are the (inductively found) topics labeled and thus linked to predefined categories (such as the regime-inherent norms in our case). This allows us to inductively explore shared meaning attributions to regime-inherent norms and to reveal norms extraneous to the regime, which would not be discovered by a deductive approach.

The climate governance literature has already provided several analyses of NDCs based on qualitative coding procedures. Some studies have revealed shared storylines or discourses (Jernnäs and Linnér 2019;Mills-Novoa and Liverman 2019). Others have scrutinized specific aspects like mitigation and adaptation targets (Tobin et al 2018;Pauw et al. 2019Pauw et al. , 2020)). Another body of research shows that a wide range of issues is covered in the NDCs (Leinaweaver and Thomson 2021;Pauw et al. 2018). We, instead, apply the inductive quantitative method of topic modeling to reveal meaning attributions to the UNFCCC's norms in all NDCs. 1 This way, we can illuminate the variety of individual norm translations as well as different norm prioritizations. We scrutinize the various norm translations by states with different country characteristics important for global climate politics, which we approximate based on membership in negotiation groups (such as the island nations group of AOSIS) and on Annex I (developed countries) versus Non-Annex I status (developing countries). These groups represent different levels of factors such as income, greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, vulnerability, and historical positions within the UNFCCC. Our approach allows us to compare norm translations between these categories of states, point to domestic factors that appear in line with particular norm translations and prioritizations, and derive implications for future global climate politics and research on norms.

Our analysis yields five main findings. First, our approach lets us identify eight norms forming the UNFCCC's norm bundle. Topic modeling shows that states translate six of these regime-inherent norms in their NDCs. This indicates the benefits in conceptualizing the international regime as containing a norm bundle. Second, we only found one instance of states translating a regime-extraneous norm. This implies that contestation in the form of open articulation of alternatives to UNF-CCC's norms is rare and specific to few countries. The UNFCCC's norm bundle's considerable staying power can be explained by its broad normative coverage since 1992, which explains why the more bottom-up NDC formulation process did not result in more references to extraneous norms. The regime's dominant discourses of liberal environmentalism and ecological modernization occupy a dominant position in NDCs, indicating the stability of the liberal order in the climate regime. Third, states translate norms in several distinct, but largely shared ways, such as renewable energy and conventional energy as distinct translations of the norm of 'mitigating GHG emissions in all relevant economic sectors'. In theoretical terms, this shows the benefits of our approach, which takes the middle ground between norm researchers focusing on full-fledged adoption and those emphasizing the novelty of each individual translation. Fourth, we find hints that norm translations vary in line with domestic context factors, such as vulnerability to climate change, GHG emission levels, income and historical positions. Our findings indicate the prominent role of domestic material factors, which adds to the norm translation literature that has so far mostly focused on domestic cultural aspects in norm translation processes. Finally, topic modeling paired with qualitative validation of representative documents is a useful method for open-ended, exploratory analyses of how norms are being translated by many actors and what priorities these different actors set. Yet, researchers interested in the nuances of (largely shared) norm translations would benefit from complementing exploratory quantitative analysis with qualitative case studies.

We proceed by introducing our conceptual framework of norm bundle translations in the next section. Subsequently, we present the UNFCCC's norm bundle, elaborate what type of document the NDCs stand for, and introduce Annex I status and negotiation groups as approximations of country-level characteristics. In the fourth section, we introduce our methods: inductive topic modeling and qualitative validation. In the fifth section, we present our results by showing both largely shared meaning attributions of states regarding individual regime-inherent or -extraneous norms and different prioritizations between norms by groups of states. We then discuss our findings and conclude with theoretical and empirical implications as well as avenues for future research.

paragraphs: International regimes contain multiple norms as part of norm bundles that formulate expectations of how parties should achieve the objective of the regime. The early norm diffusion literature already acknowledged the idea to understand norms 'as part of a highly structured social context […] of interlocking and interwoven norms' (Finnemore 1996: 161). Yet, they solely analyzed the role of individual norms. For our understanding of norm bundles, we build upon the definition by Lantis and Wunderlich (2018: 571), who define norm clusters as 'collections of aligned, but distinct, norms or principles that relate to a common, overarching issue area; they address different aspects and contain specific normative obligations'. While Lantis and Wunderlich formulate the norm cluster with regard to an issue area, we conceptualize the norm bundle with regard to an international regime to allow for a further specification. We define a norm bundle as a set of distinct norms that jointly serve to define the normative obligations for parties to fulfill an international regime's overarching objective. These regime-inherent norms represent 'shared expectations about appropriate behavior held by a community of actors' (Finnemore 1996: 22). They, hence, include a 'sense of oughtness' (Finnemore and Sikkink 1998: 891-892;Jurkovitch 2020: 694-695), and are aligned together in the form of the norm bundle to fulfill an international regime's overarching objective. 2Our conceptualization differs from Winston (2018: 647), who perceives 'norm clusters' to be a 'bounded collection of interrelated specific problems, values, and behaviors'. Her norm cluster approach aspires to map the entire range of behaviors in relation to identified problem(s) and underlying value(s) that are perceived as appropriate by a community of actors (Winston 2018: 647-648). Our norm bundle approach instead focuses on the norms institutionalized in an international treaty as a starting point to scrutinize how states translate them (irrespective of whether their particular translations are perceived as acceptable to the community of actors or not). In this regard, we concur with Lantis and Wunderlich (2018: 576), who define the norms ex ante to the behavior of agents. In their view, these norms are 'integral components of international regimes' often reflecting 'package deals' (Lantis and Wunderlich 2018: 578) made in international negotiations.

Similar notions to our perspective on norm bundles can be found in other works, for example in the conceptualization of the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) as a 'cluster of interrelated norms grouped into a unified and coherent framework' (Fukuda-Parr and Hulme 2011: 18), or in the conceptualization of the Responsibility to Protect as a 'bundle of closely related but still distinct norms' (Bloomfield and Scott 2017: 3). However, similar to the early norm diffusion scholars, these approaches remain committed to a stable understanding of individual norms of the norm bundle or cluster that can only be rejected or accepted (for a similar critique, see Winston 2018: 644-646). We, instead, embrace a more flexible understanding of the norms embedded in an international regime's norm bundle. As international regimes reflect a range of normative perspectives about appropriate behavior, what they actually mean for the different actors is subject to their own meaning attributions and can, therefore, be quite flexible and evolving (see also Höhne 2022;Krook and True 2010: 109-110). Thus, we rely on the norm translation literature in order to study how agents engage in different meaning attributions of individual norms.

Given the nature of international regimes as products of complex negotiations, we argue that norm bundles reflect different normative perspectives about appropriate behavior. As a consequence, parties will not seek to uniformly engage with each norm simultaneously and will not interpret them in the same way. When states are faced with normative obligations under an international regime -that they may very well have negotiated themselves earlier -they must decide how to translate them in relation to their own domestic context. This is an insight from the literature on (cultural) translation (Chakrabarty 2000;Bhabha 2004Bhabha [1994]]): The meaning of international norms is always subject to interpretation by the (domestic) actors who engage with them. 3The emerging literature on norm translation builds on and expands previous works on norm socialization, localization, and contestation (Risse et al. 1999;Acharya 2004;Wiener 2004Wiener , 2018)). 4 Norm translation scholarship emphasizes the changing meaning of norms when agents engage with them. For example, some previous norm translation approaches allow for the modification of international norms in domestic processes of implementation (Zimmermann 2017: 55). Others include insights from the literature on cultural translation to show that norm translation not only changes the norm but also the local context itself (Berger 2017: 24-25). We do not focus on the domestic implementation of international norms (such as Betts and Orchard 2014), but instead scrutinize the discursive implementation pledges that reflect different meaning attributions to the existing norm bundle of the international regime (as stated in its international treaty), potentially alongside other norms external to the regime.

Cultural translation research notes the fluidity of social concepts that are changed and adapted through processes of meaning attributions by concerned agents. This strand emphasizes that agents do not merely reproduce the original meaning of social concepts, but always engage in translation processes (cf., Bachmann-Medick 2009: 8). When actors encounter and engage with international norms, they do so based on their own 'pre-existing concepts [...] through which [international norms…] get translated and configured differently' (Chakrabarty 2000: xii). This may result in 'cultural hybridity' (Bhabha 2004(Bhabha [1994]]: 5), with individual translations representing something new (Bhabha 2004(Bhabha [1994]]: 313; Capan et al. 2021: 7). Yet, not every norm translation is completely idiosyncratic: States in similar contexts (such as with a quest for economic development or with high vulnerability to climate change) may very well engage in largely similar forms of norm translations. This means that we can find commonalities in norm translations that go beyond idiosyncratic forms of meaning attribution. For understanding how norm translations vary between states, it thus seems beneficial to compare them based on states' particular country-level characteristics. The literature on norm translation has already pointed towards the role of domestic cultural factors in norm translation (cf, Berger 2017; Zimmermann 2017), which we expand by scrutinizing domestic material factors as well.

This framework allows us to study the variation in how actors interpret the different norms of a regime's norm bundle based on their own domestic cultural and/or material context. While we do not directly observe and study translation processes between the international and the national level as such, we analyze their results: When state parties submit implementation pledges in the context of an international regime, they express which meanings they attribute to the different regime-inherent norms or to norms external to the regime. We analyze both the translations of individual norms (inherent or external to the regime) as well as the translation of the entire norm bundle in the form of norm prioritizations based on the relative emphases between different norms.

First, states translate norms on their own in the form of individual norm translations. When these translations concern norms embedded in the international treaty, we call this regime-inherent norm translations. States may have different interpretations of the same regime-inherent norm due to its unspecific wording in the international treaty and because of the different domestic contexts from which states try to make sense of the norm. However, states' meaning attributions may also involve norms from outside of the regime's norm bundle. Actors can add normative aspects based on their own domestic context in their implementation pledges that go beyond the ones already incorporated in the international treaty. This is what we call regimeextraneous norm translations. 5Second, state parties do not necessarily translate all norms of a regime's norm bundle equally. When norms reflect different or even competing priorities, states are likely to focus more on those that align with their domestic context. They, hence, may choose to prioritize some of the regime's norms at the expense of others (up to the point of omission). This norm prioritization (in which some norms are emphasized relative to others) serves to assess how states translate the norm bundle as a whole.

However, similar to the early norm diffusion scholars, these approaches remain committed to a stable understanding of individual norms of the norm bundle or cluster that can only be rejected or accepted (for a similar critique, see Winston 2018: 644-646). We, instead, embrace a more flexible understanding of the norms embedded in an international regime's norm bundle. As international regimes reflect a range of normative perspectives about appropriate behavior, what they actually mean for the different actors is subject to their own meaning attributions and can, therefore, be quite flexible and evolving (see also Höhne 2022;Krook and True 2010: 109-110). Thus, we rely on the norm translation literature in order to study how agents engage in different meaning attributions of individual norms.

Given the nature of international regimes as products of complex negotiations, we argue that norm bundles reflect different normative perspectives about appropriate behavior. As a consequence, parties will not seek to uniformly engage with each norm simultaneously and will not interpret them in the same way. When states are faced with normative obligations under an international regime -that they may very well have negotiated themselves earlier -they must decide how to translate them in relation to their own domestic context. This is an insight from the literature on (cultural) translation (Chakrabarty 2000;Bhabha 2004Bhabha [1994]]): The meaning of international norms is always subject to interpretation by the (domestic) actors who engage with them. 3The emerging literature on norm translation builds on and expands previous works on norm socialization, localization, and contestation (Risse et al. 1999;Acharya 2004;Wiener 2004Wiener , 2018)). 4 Norm translation scholarship emphasizes the changing meaning of norms when agents engage with them. For example, some previous norm translation approaches allow for the modification of international norms in domestic processes of implementation (Zimmermann 2017: 55). Others include insights from the literature on cultural translation to show that norm translation not only changes the norm but also the local context itself (Berger 2017: 24-25). We do not focus on the domestic implementation of international norms (such as Betts and Orchard 2014), but instead scrutinize the discursive implementation pledges that reflect different meaning attributions to the existing norm bundle of the international regime (as stated in its international treaty), potentially alongside other norms external to the regime.

Cultural translation research notes the fluidity of social concepts that are changed and adapted through processes of meaning attributions by concerned agents. This strand emphasizes that agents do not merely reproduce the original meaning of social concepts, but always engage in translation processes (cf., Bachmann-Medick 2009: 8). When actors encounter and engage with international norms, they do so based on their own 'pre-existing concepts [...] through which [international norms…] get translated and configured differently' (Chakrabarty 2000: xii). This may result in 'cultural hybridity' (Bhabha 2004(Bhabha [1994]]: 5), with individual translations representing something new (Bhabha 2004(Bhabha [1994]]: 313; Capan et al. 2021: 7). Yet, not every norm translation is completely idiosyncratic: States in similar contexts (such as with a quest for economic development or with high vulnerability to climate change) may very well engage in largely similar forms of norm translations. This means that we can find commonalities in norm translations that go beyond idiosyncratic forms of meaning attribution. For understanding how norm translations vary between states, it thus seems beneficial to compare them based on states' particular country-level characteristics. The literature on norm translation has already pointed towards the role of domestic cultural factors in norm translation (cf, Berger 2017; Zimmermann 2017), which we expand by scrutinizing domestic material factors as well.

This framework allows us to study the variation in how actors interpret the different norms of a regime's norm bundle based on their own domestic cultural and/or material context. While we do not directly observe and study translation processes between the international and the national level as such, we analyze their results: When state parties submit implementation pledges in the context of an international regime, they express which meanings they attribute to the different regime-inherent norms or to norms external to the regime. We analyze both the translations of individual norms (inherent or external to the regime) as well as the translation of the entire norm bundle in the form of norm prioritizations based on the relative emphases between different norms.

First, states translate norms on their own in the form of individual norm translations. When these translations concern norms embedded in the international treaty, we call this regime-inherent norm translations. States may have different interpretations of the same regime-inherent norm due to its unspecific wording in the international treaty and because of the different domestic contexts from which states try to make sense of the norm. However, states' meaning attributions may also involve norms from outside of the regime's norm bundle. Actors can add normative aspects based on their own domestic context in their implementation pledges that go beyond the ones already incorporated in the international treaty. This is what we call regimeextraneous norm translations. 5Second, state parties do not necessarily translate all norms of a regime's norm bundle equally. When norms reflect different or even competing priorities, states are likely to focus more on those that align with their domestic context. They, hence, may choose to prioritize some of the regime's norms at the expense of others (up to the point of omission). This norm prioritization (in which some norms are emphasized relative to others) serves to assess how states translate the norm bundle as a whole.

sections.header_raw: The UNFCCC norm bundle and the role of NDCs
sections.header_norm: the unfccc norm bundle and the role of ndcs
paragraphs: The UNFCCC norm bundle

The Convention, signed in 1992, defines the objective of the UNFCCC as to prevent dangerous climate change (UNFCCC 1992: Article 2). Article 3 then provides a more detailed set of eight 'principles', which we conceptualize as UNFCCC's regime-inherent norms, as they describe shared expectations about appropriate behavior by which the parties to the convention 'shall be guided' (Article 3):

(1) Mitigation based on equity and common but differentiated responsibilities and respective capabilities comes first (Article 3.1): 'The Parties should protect the climate system for the benefit of present and future generations of humankind, on the basis of equity and in accordance with their common but differentiated responsibilities and respective capabilities' (in short: mitigation responsibilities).

For developed countries this includes the normative request to 'take the lead in combating climate change and the adverse effects thereof' (Article 3.1). (2) Article 3.2 adds to consider the 'specific needs and special circumstances of developing country Parties, especially those that are particularly vulnerable to the adverse effects of climate change' and those 'that would have to bear a disproportionate or abnormal burden under the Convention' (in short: specific needs).

(3) The regime's third norm (Article 3.3) states that '[t]he Parties should take precautionary measures to anticipate, prevent or minimize the causes of climate change and mitigate its adverse effects' (in short: precautionary actions). (4) It is followed by the notion of cost-effectiveness (Article 3.3): 'policies and measures to deal with climate change should be cost-effective so as to ensure global benefits at the lowest possible cost' (in short: cost-effectiveness). These regime-inherent norms are the product of negotiations leading to the 1992 Convention, when states with different normative goals ensured that their perspectives were included in Article 3. Some of them appear to be conceptualized in a synergistic manner, such as when economic development is seen as essential for mitigating climate change. This motivated Steven Bernstein to emphasize that the UNFCCC reflects the spirit of liberal environmentalism, in which 'international environmental protection [rests] on the promotion and maintenance of a liberal economic order (Bernstein 2002: 1). Discourse analysts have also identified ecological modernization as dominant in the climate regime, emphasizing the 'compatibility of economic growth and environmental protection' (Bäckstrand and Lövbrand 2006: 52). Yet, other scholars have pointed to the conflictive relationship between emission reduction and economic development in practice (Harris and Symons 2013). In general, the regime-inherent norms have different levels of precision. While some are composed as general rights (economic development) or reflect more broad normative expectations (mitigation responsibilities) that require further operationalization, others are more specific and straightforward to interpret (mitigation sectors).

Further operationalizing the norm bundle, the 1992 Convention specifies that developed countries listed in Annex I shall adopt policies and take actions to mitigate climate change and shall demonstrate that they take the lead in this regard in order to fulfill their mitigation responsibilities (Article 4.2(a)). They shall also provide international funding and technologies to developing countries as a support for their obligations (Article 4.3) and for adaptation (Article 4.4), due to their specific needs and circumstances (see also Article 4.8, 4.9). Developing countries, usually referred to as Non-Annex I countries, were only asked to take mitigation and adaptation actions in accordance with their mitigation responsibilities and specific development priorities (Article 4.1 and 12.1). In practice, developing countries emphasized the responsibility of the Annex I countries to take the lead on mitigation responsibilities, while all parties prioritized economic development (Jinnah 2017: 294;Vogler 2018: 21).

Since the adoption of the UNFCCC, states have negotiated the operationalization and implementation of its objective and norms at annual COPs (Bulkeley and Newell 2010). Adaptation and specific needs in the form of finance for developing countries did not play a strong role in the UNFCCC in the 1990s, yet were taken up much more since the early 2000s along with the continuously dominant mitigation focus (Kuyper et al. 2018: 355-359). In 1997, the Kyoto Protocol introduced an obligation for developed countries to undertake legally binding quantitative emission cuts (UNFCCC 1998: Article 3.1), as they had to take the lead according to their mitigation responsibilities. This included international carbon markets that indicate the importance of cost-effectiveness in the regime. The acceptance of 'Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Actions' (NAMAs) by developing countries at the Bali COP in 2007 symbolized a normative shift by developing countries to accept domestic mitigation efforts as a new operationalization of their mitigation responsibilities (Höhne 2018: 131, Höhne 2022). Over time, more emphasis was placed on bottomup elements, such as voluntary pledges by all parties, in addition to top-down goals, such as in Copenhagen in 2009 (Bodansky 2010: 236). The 2015 Paris Agreement solidified this development. It aims to reach a (top-down) global warming goal by means of (bottom-up) NDCs that are meant to progress over time (UNFCCC 2016: Article 2-4).

paragraphs: At the 2014 Lima COP, some loose UNFCCC guidelines emerged on the 'Intended' NDCs to be submitted by states prior to the Paris Conference (Pauw and Klein 2020: 406). 6(1) All parties were requested to indicate their contribution to reaching the objective of the 1992 Convention of Article 2, which shall represent a progression compared to previous efforts (UNFCCC 2015: 3).

(2) NDCs were to include 'quantifiable information on the reference point (including, as appropriate, a base year), time frames (...), scope and coverage, planning processes, assumptions and methodological approaches' (UNFCCC 2015: 3) for GHG emissions accounting. Thereby, parties were requested to consider their contribution's fairness and ambition considering their national circumstances. This operationalizes the regime-inherent norm of mitigation responsibilities. In addition, the term national circumstances also hints at the role of cost-effectiveness, specific needs or economic development.

(3) An exception was made for LDCs and small island developing states, which were invited to communicate plans and measures for low carbon development that also reflect their special circumstances (UNFCCC 2015: 3). Instead of operationalizing mitigation responsibilities in the form of concrete targets, these states were thus invited to address the regime-inherent norm of mitigation sectors. As they could choose their actions in a way that reflects their specific circumstances and development, we can further expect to find operationalizations of regimeinherent norms of specific needs and/or economic development. (4) All parties were invited to include adaptation plans in their 'Intended' NDCs (UNFCCC 2015: 3), underlining the additional focus on this regime-inherent norm.

The Lima guidelines thus addressed only some of UNFCCC's regime-inherent norms: mitigation responsibilities, mitigation sectors, and adaptation. Solely the references to national or special circumstances indicate that they could also ponder on economic development, specific needs or cost-effectiveness. Yet, no guidance was given that would have hinted at a reference to precautionary actions or free trade. The non-binding Lima guidelines are relevant because most states submitted their 'Intended' NDCs before the Paris COP without knowing the outcome of the negotiations. After the successful completion of the conference, very few countries used the chance to alter their documents. In most cases, the NDCs are exact copies of previously submitted 'Intended' NDCs (Pauw and Klein 2020: 407). While the Paris Agreement provided additional instructions on how to prepare NDCs, these provisions did not have an effect on the already submitted documents. In sum, the NDCs contain states' implementation pledges to achieve the Convention objective up to 2030. In a way, they are a hybrid kind of document placed in between the national and the international level, as NDC drafters had to keep both international and domestic audiences in mind.

(4) All parties were invited to include adaptation plans in their 'Intended' NDCs (UNFCCC 2015: 3), underlining the additional focus on this regime-inherent norm.

The Lima guidelines thus addressed only some of UNFCCC's regime-inherent norms: mitigation responsibilities, mitigation sectors, and adaptation. Solely the references to national or special circumstances indicate that they could also ponder on economic development, specific needs or cost-effectiveness. Yet, no guidance was given that would have hinted at a reference to precautionary actions or free trade. The non-binding Lima guidelines are relevant because most states submitted their 'Intended' NDCs before the Paris COP without knowing the outcome of the negotiations. After the successful completion of the conference, very few countries used the chance to alter their documents. In most cases, the NDCs are exact copies of previously submitted 'Intended' NDCs (Pauw and Klein 2020: 407). While the Paris Agreement provided additional instructions on how to prepare NDCs, these provisions did not have an effect on the already submitted documents. In sum, the NDCs contain states' implementation pledges to achieve the Convention objective up to 2030. In a way, they are a hybrid kind of document placed in between the national and the international level, as NDC drafters had to keep both international and domestic audiences in mind.

paragraphs: Given the wide normative scope of the UNFCCC, we expect variation in individual norm translations as well as prioritization. As already mentioned, our conceptual framework presumes that states are likely to opt for similar norm translations when they share characteristics regarding their domestic context. Vice versa, states with divergent backgrounds may differ in their individual translation and prioritization. As an approximation of country-specific context, we use the UNFCCC's distinction in Annex I (developed) versus Non-Annex I (developing) countries as well as negotiation groups that bring together states with similar specific country-level characteristics, such as their vulnerability to climate change, and historical positions within UNFCCC climate politics. The groups thus represent categories of states based on multiple background factors, allowing us to compare translations between different domestic contexts.

As an approximation of country-specific context, we, firstly, compare norm translations across UNFCCC annex status. This provides a combination of several material domestic context factors like GHG emissions, vulnerability and income. Annex status also covers countries' historical positions and roles in climate politics, which have a material basis but can also be considered as a more cultural, ideational factor due to their path-dependency. Annex I countries are income-rich, have relatively low vulnerabilities to climate change, have high per capita emissions, and are responsible for the build-up of GHG over a long time period. Under the Kyoto Regime, these industrialized states had the obligation to reduce their absolute GHG emissions compared to their 1990 levels, representing one form of similar historical position.

Non-Annex I countries bring together all developing countries with relatively higher degrees of vulnerability than most Annex I states and mostly low to medium levels of income and emissions (except for the emerging and most oil-producing economies). Under the Kyoto Regime, those states did not have any obligation to reduce GHG emissions, rejected absolute emission reductions and underlined the importance of economic growth for their development, indicating some historical positions shared by most of them. Yet, since the 2009 Copenhagen COP, some of them have started to pledge relative emission targets voluntarily (based on business as usual emission developments or GDP growth). Even though all states are obligated to submit NDCs under the Paris Agreement, we expect that the different state characteristics and different historical positions between Annex I and Non-Annex I countries continue to shape the norm translations articulated in the NDCs.

Secondly, we are interested in more specific state characteristics to illuminate distinct norm translations by sub-categories of states. As an approximation of these more specific domestic contexts, we use the membership in UNFCCC negotiation groups. Negotiation groups have been historically formed by states with shared characteristics that are important for their engagement with climate change, such as their vulnerability to climate change, income, GHG emissions or historical positions on climate change. Consequently, we assume that states within the same category may engage in similar norm translations. 7The negotiation groups European Union (EU), Umbrella Group, and Environmental Integrity Group (EIG) include Annex I states with high levels of income and per-capita emissions as well as relatively low vulnerability. Yet, they differ in their historical positions on the climate regime. Historically, the EU has often comprised the most ambitious developed countries. In contrast, the Umbrella Group (with the United States and Australia) also includes several states that have been or continue to be mitigation-reluctant due to their particularly high per-capita emissions and strong reliance on fossil fuels. They mostly agree on the usage of market mechanisms and emphasize cost-effectiveness. EIG brings together several OECD countries, including two Non-Annex I countries, Mexico and South Korea, which together support ambitious mitigation actions in the global North and South (Klöck et al. 2021;Yamin and Depledge 2004). We, therefore, assume to find norm translations by their member states in the NDCs that are similar to these historical positions.

The Non-Annex I groups can be grouped in four broad categories (see Table 1). The first category includes three different negotiation groups (BASIC, LMDC, OPEC) with states that have medium to high levels of income and emissions and a shared historical position of mitigation-reluctance. Emerging economies with medium income and high total emissions can be found in the BASIC group (Brazil, South Africa, India and China). In the past, they have rejected quantitative mitigation commitments as part of their historical position. One particular group also includes states with high levels of income and per-capita emissions whose economies are heavily based on fossil fuel production (OPEC). In the past, OPEC has slowed and watered down the negotiation process, as they feared the consequences of ambitious climate actions for their business models, indicating a very mitigationreluctant historical position. The Like-minded developing countries (LMDC) group brings together first-or second-tier emerging economies (India, China, Indonesia, Malaysia, Pakistan, Vietnam) with oil-producing states (Iraq, Saudi Arabia, Venezuela), and some other developing countries (incl. Bolivia). It represents a melange of countries with medium to high levels of emissions and income, and in some cases an economic reliance on fossil fuel production. Their historical position outlines a strict differentiation depending on Annex status: They emphasize Annex I countries' responsibility for mitigation, are reluctant concerning quantitative commitments for themselves, emphasize the need for economic growth and require international support (Klöck et al. 2021).

By contrast, the second category includes two groups that are based on a shared sense of high vulnerability to climate change. The Least Developed Countries (LDCs) are especially vulnerable to climate change, have only low total emissions, and have weak capacities to take action on climate change due to their low income. They are joined in this category by the Alliance of Small Island States (AOSIS), which are particularly vulnerable to sea level rise, among other issues, have small economies with middle to high income, and have followed the historical position 8) EIG ( 5) BASIC ( 4) LMDC ( 23) OPEC ( 10) LDC ( 44) AOSIS ( 39) ALBA ( 6) AILAC ( 7)
of demanding more ambitious global climate actions (Yamin and Depledge 2004;Blaxekjaer and Nielsen 2015;Watts and Depledge 2018;Klöck et al. 2021).

The third and fourth category includes two regional groupings that place emphasis on explicitly political goals, representing different cultural backgrounds and historical positions. The Bolivarian Alliance for the Peoples of Our America (ALBA) is a developing countries' group connected by its anti-capitalist cultural stance and a more mitigation-reluctant historical position. In contrast, the Independent Association of Latin America and the Caribbean (AILAC) developing countries has followed a historical position proposing ambitious mitigation actions and constructive negotiations between the global North and South (Watts and Depledge 2018: 1-2, 6). 8 To summarize, UNFCCC annex status and group membership stand for countrylevel characteristics. This includes material domestic factors, such as vulnerability to climate change, GHG emission level, income or fossil-fuel production. Moreover, we take into account states' historical positions. Lastly, we cover group-specific cultural backgrounds, such as anti-capitalism. By comparing NDC contents between these categories of states, we can explore how domestic context factors are linked to particular norm translations and norm prioritizations.

sections.header_raw: Data and methods: NDCs and topic modeling
sections.header_norm: data and methods ndcs and topic modeling
paragraphs: The text corpus for our analysis consists of all first NDCs submitted by the parties to the UNFCCC up until February 2020. These were downloaded from the official UNFCCC website. 9 Across all cases, we rely on the most current document. Since all 28 (at the time) European Union member states submitted the same NDC along with the EU itself, we have 158 different documents to analyze. For 20 documents, there was no English version available. In those cases, we relied on DeepL for automatic translation.

Despite being submitted in the same context, the NDCs are very different in length. The shorter documents are just a couple of pages long and rely on tables, while some countries submitted dozens of pages with in-depth explanations. Wordiness seems related to Annex I status, with Annex I countries submitting shorter documents (Fig. 1). Historically, they were used to reporting quantitative mitigation targets under the Kyoto Protocol. To ensure that document length does not influence our results, topic models were trained on individual sections of NDCs as indicated by document headings, resulting in 815 units of text (see Technical Annex). 8 We excluded some groups from the analysis. This includes groups that reflect a shared geography but no common historical positions or state characteristics, such as the Arab States. Moreover, we excluded the G77, as it covers all developing countries and is therefore very similar to the Non-Annex I status. Lastly, we refrained from including the Coalition for Rainforest Nations (CfRN) due to its very specific forestry focus, particularly on REDD+, while their members otherwise often do not share common positions (cf. Yamin and Depledge 2004;Klöck et al. 2021). 9 See https:// www4. unfccc. int/ sites/ NDCSt aging/ Pages/ All. aspx (download date: September 2020).

To analyze this text corpus, we use an open-ended quantitative method -topic modeling. 10 Rather than classifying text based on predefined categories, this allows us to detect patterns of norm translations inductively: Topic modeling is a method for identifying patterns of words that commonly occur together, which are conceptualized as (thematic) topics. We use the most common variant called Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) (Blei et al. 2003). This statistical technique analyzes word frequencies and creates groups of words based on how coherently they appear together in the same documents (based on Gibbs sampling). 11 These collections of related words are called topics.

To find the number of topics (k) most suitable to analyze the available data, we chose an approach that combines quantitative and qualitative insights. Statistical techniques suggest that at least 15 topics would be necessary. We then tried different configurations and qualitatively identified 15 topics as the optimal choice. To ensure that our results are reproducible and representative, we ran 20 iterations with identical settings, only changing the random seed. Out of the most typical topic models, we picked the model that combines high overall reproducibility with a topic configuration that provides the most substantive information (for details and references, see Technical Annex).

Importantly, this part of the analysis does not involve prior assumptions about which keywords might define particular topics. Instead, topics are inductively 10 All stages of preprocessing and computational analysis were done using the iLCM platform. The iLCM is a text mining infrastructure for the use in the social sciences that provides a graphical interface to use various R packages. See http:// ilcm. infor matik. uni-leipz ig. de/. 11 To avoid distortions due to country-specific terms (e.g., the names of cities or government bodies), we only include words that occur in more than one NDC.

discovered by the statistical model based on patterns in the text. Each topic is then manually labeled based on the 30 most frequent and representative words and a close reading of relevant sections of the most representative documents for each topic (see Fig. 2). 12 Having assigned labels to the generated topics, we then qualitatively match them to the predefined regime-inherent norms and identify additional norms external to the regime (Fig. 3, next section).

We do not only link topics to norms but also to NDCs and, thus, states. When a topic takes up more than one fifteenth (i.e., 1/k) of a document, we treat this as evidence that the respective NDC is associated with the topic (since, in technical terms, its probability value theta exceeds the threshold one would find for an equal distribution of all topics). This allows us to compare how widely each topic (and thus a norm translation) is shared across all NDCs. The topic modeling produced 15 topics that are discussed to different extents. 'Relative emission pledges', 'UNFCCC collaboration', and 'adaptation governance' are the topics the most widely shared (see Fig. 2 Topics and their keywords. Note. Bar length and numbers indicate how many NDCs are above the threshold value for the topic. The right side lists the top 10 words for each topic plus additional, representative terms in italics 12 Choosing the lambda parameter for summarizing each topic involves a trade-off between the most specific words for each topic (which can be very rare) and those that are more frequent in the overall text corpus (and thus less useful to differentiate between topics). We have identified a lambda value of 0.3 as optimal to separate the nuances of different topics. Note that this choice only concerns the words used for interpreting the topics, not their initial formation (which is based on the distribution of all words). Fig. 2). The NDCs with the highest probability values for the corresponding topic serves as representative cases for the qualitative interpretation.

Moreover, to assess similarities and differences in norm translation and prioritization and to explore how domestic context factors are linked to them, we compare the prevalence of topics between groups of countries, such as Annex I status and negotiation groups at the time of the Paris COP (data from Pearce and Yeo 2015). 13To this end, we calculate the mean probability values (theta) for each topic among the NDCs belonging to each group. This topic prevalence indicates what percentage of their NDCs the group memberson average -allocate to the respective topic, which we see as indicative of how much importance they assign to it (see Figs. 4 and 5 in the next section). Using membership in these groups as approximations for country-level characteristics, we can thus link particular norm translations and prioritization to the domestic context factors.

Lastly, we refrained from including the Coalition for Rainforest Nations (CfRN) due to its very specific forestry focus, particularly on REDD+, while their members otherwise often do not share common positions (cf. Yamin and Depledge 2004;Klöck et al. 2021). 9 See https:// www4. unfccc. int/ sites/ NDCSt aging/ Pages/ All. aspx (download date: September 2020).

To analyze this text corpus, we use an open-ended quantitative method -topic modeling. 10 Rather than classifying text based on predefined categories, this allows us to detect patterns of norm translations inductively: Topic modeling is a method for identifying patterns of words that commonly occur together, which are conceptualized as (thematic) topics. We use the most common variant called Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) (Blei et al. 2003). This statistical technique analyzes word frequencies and creates groups of words based on how coherently they appear together in the same documents (based on Gibbs sampling). 11 These collections of related words are called topics.

To find the number of topics (k) most suitable to analyze the available data, we chose an approach that combines quantitative and qualitative insights. Statistical techniques suggest that at least 15 topics would be necessary. We then tried different configurations and qualitatively identified 15 topics as the optimal choice. To ensure that our results are reproducible and representative, we ran 20 iterations with identical settings, only changing the random seed. Importantly, this part of the analysis does not involve prior assumptions about which keywords might define particular topics. Instead, topics are inductively 10 All stages of preprocessing and computational analysis were done using the iLCM platform. See http:// ilcm. infor matik. uni-leipz ig. de/. 11 To avoid distortions due to country-specific terms (e.g., the names of cities or government bodies), we only include words that occur in more than one NDC.

discovered by the statistical model based on patterns in the text. We do not only link topics to norms but also to NDCs and, thus, states. When a topic takes up more than one fifteenth (i.e., 1/k) of a document, we treat this as evidence that the respective NDC is associated with the topic (since, in technical terms, its probability value theta exceeds the threshold one would find for an equal distribution of all topics). This allows us to compare how widely each topic (and thus a norm translation) is shared across all NDCs. The topic modeling produced 15 topics that are discussed to different extents. 'Relative emission pledges', 'UNFCCC collaboration', and 'adaptation governance' are the topics the most widely shared (see Fig. 2 Topics and their keywords. Note. Bar length and numbers indicate how many NDCs are above the threshold value for the topic. The right side lists the top 10 words for each topic plus additional, representative terms in italics 12 Choosing the lambda parameter for summarizing each topic involves a trade-off between the most specific words for each topic (which can be very rare) and those that are more frequent in the overall text corpus (and thus less useful to differentiate between topics). We have identified a lambda value of 0.3 as optimal to separate the nuances of different topics. Note that this choice only concerns the words used for interpreting the topics, not their initial formation (which is based on the distribution of all words). Fig. 2). The NDCs with the highest probability values for the corresponding topic serves as representative cases for the qualitative interpretation.

sections.header_raw: Norm translation and prioritization in the NDCs
sections.header_norm: norm translation and prioritization in the ndcs
paragraphs: In this section, we investigate in which ways and how prominently the UNFCCC's different regime-inherent norms have been translated in the NDCs. That requires us to draw connections between the norms embedded in the regime, which we identified ex ante based on Article 3 of the Convention, and the topics, which were discovered inductively by analyzing the NDCs. The arrows in Fig. 3 indicate which norm takes center stage in each topic, also including the rare instance of regime-extraneous norms. Several regime-inherent norms are addressed in multiple topics as different norm translations. The most frequent ones are: mitigation responsibilities, mitigation sectors and adaptation (with links to four topics each). After illuminating the different individual norm translations, we present the domestic context factors that we found to be associated with particular norm prioritizations.

sections.paper_id: Höhne et al. - 2023 - Translating the norm bundle of an international regime states’ pledges on climate change around the
paragraphs: States have translated the regime-inherent norm of mitigation responsibilities in four different ways, captured by four distinct topics on absolute emission targets, relative emission targets, UNFCCC collaboration for global mitigation efforts, and concrete domestic climate governance actions. The first topic of 'emission pledges and markets' indicates that 82 countries have translated the mitigation responsibilities norm by referring to economy-wide commitments based on IPCC methodologies (see Fig. 2 for the key words). This topic, hence, contains the kind of absolute GHG emission reduction targets usually employed by developed countries since the Kyoto Protocol, indicating their approach of taking the lead in limiting climate change. Additionally, it includes translations of the regime-inherent norm of costeffectiveness, as evident by the reference to the usage of market mechanisms and carbon credits. The utilization of carbon market mechanisms is presumed to channel funding for mitigation to the most cost-effective actions and has been a cornerstone of global climate politics since the Kyoto Protocol (cf., Bulkeley and Newell 2010: 31-32).

All next three topics solely translate the norm of mitigation responsibilities. In distinction to absolute emission reduction targets, 134 states have translated the norm in the form of the 'relative emission pledges' topic. Relevant words indicate that these countries have submitted relative GHG emission targets. This includes pledges conditional on financial aid as well as unconditional emission reduction targets, defined in relation to either business as usual (BAU) emissions or economic growth (i.e., emission intensity). The third form of translating the norm of mitigation responsibilities can be found in the 'UNFCCC collaboration' topic, indicating countries' framing of their contributions to the global efforts on mitigating climate change. It includes references to the overall UNFCCC governance architecture (convention, parties), but also to different reflections on ambition and responsibility regarding own mitigation actions (common [but] differentiated responsibilities, ambitious, effort) by 112 states. The fourth topic represents the translation of mitigation responsibilities into 'domestic governance'. Here, the 71 NDCs largely refer to institutions and processes for the preparation, implementation, monitoring, and evaluation of concrete domestic climate actions.

The regime-inherent norm of mitigation sectors was translated in four different ways as evident in topics that list four distinct forms of concrete mitigation actions. Two topics indicate very different norm translations with respect to the production of energy: In the 'renewable energy' topic, 75 states discuss measures such as the promotion of solar and wind energy as a shift away from fossil fuels. Actions to address energy efficiency (e.g. in buildings) or to shift to low-carbon transport also appear in this topic, but much less prominently, as indicated by the most representative documents. A completely different way of addressing energy production as a translation of mitigation sectors is captured by the 'conventional energy' topic. Here, 25 states refer to nuclear energy or claim the 'clean use of coal' (China's NDC, p. 7) by increasing the efficiency in coal-based power generation. Less frequent issues appear here as well, such as transport-related mitigation actions like promoting efficiency improvements of vehicles and the promotion of railways. Two further topics address different takes on rural economic mitigation sectors. 69 countries focus on mitigation actions like reducing deforestation and degradation as well as promoting afforestation in the 'forestry actions' topic. The forest-energy nexus is addressed, too. In contrast, the 'land use and anti-capitalism' topic discusses mitigation actions on cattle ranching and land management, such as most prominently in the case of Uruguay.

States translate the regime-inherent norm of adaptation in four different ways, captured by four distinct topics. The 'adaptation governance' topic indicates how 111 states translate the norm in a cross-sectoral way by addressing resilience, and the mainstreaming of adaptation in development plans. The topic includes language on the governance instruments that states plan to use to adapt to climate change impacts, including National Adaptation Programmes of Action (NAPA). Three further topics translate the norm based on more specific types of circumstances. The 'water-related disasters' topic is emphasized by 64 countries. The words in this topic refer to particular vulnerabilities to climate change based on sea level rise and extreme weather events, such as heavy rainfall, flooding, cyclones, and drought. While this topic only sparsely contains actions or plans of adaptation to address those climate impacts, it includes (natural and societal) risks assessments and sets the priorities for more concrete adaptation interventions in the NDCs of the most representative cases. Illustrating another priority related to water, 51 states use the 'water scarcity management' topic to address specific interventions responding to vulnerabilities in the water supply, including both the needs of the general population and irrigation for farming. Less prominently, adaptation in the health sector due to the increase of diseases appears here as well. Focusing on a different and even narrower set of circumstances, 21 states translate the adaptation norm in the form of 'pastoralism and land management'. The most representative cases refer to the need for adaptation in the areas of sustainable land management (SLM), animal husbandry, and agroecology.

Two further topics indicate distinct ways of translating the regime-inherent norm of specific needs of developing countries. The 'international support' topic is emphasized by 54 states. They demand financial support, GCF (Green Climate Fund) projects, capacity building, or technology transfer to cover either the costs of climate actions and/or to meet the climate-related needs of developing countries. In contrast, the 'post-war rural measures' topic is only addressed by five countries. They refer to a very narrow version of specific needs related to special post-war circumstances in rural developing countries.

The regime-inherent norm of economic development is translated in the 'economic growth' topic. 72 states emphasize issues like the need for economic growth to alleviate poverty and to reduce unemployment of their growing populations. This can include shifts to more sustainable development pathways (e.g. Papua New Guinea) or the reliance on conventional fossil-fuel based development trajectories (e.g. Pakistan).

In sum, states have translated six of the eight regime-inherent norms of the Convention into their NDCs. This includes all the three norms explicitly highlighted in the 2014 Lima guidelines and three more that could broadly be perceived to fall under the categories of national and special circumstances. This indicates the strong normative path shaping role of those regime-inherent norms for the entirety of NDCs. Several of these norms have been translated in distinct and contrasting ways, such as renewable versus conventional energy or absolute versus relative emission reduction targets. We did not find references to either precautionary actions or free trade. 14 Those two regime-inherent norms reflected the mindset at the time of the Convention's drafting. While scientific uncertainty was reduced drastically with the periodical IPCC reports, free trade restrictions as carbon border adjustment measures were not a matter of discussion around 2015.

In contrast to the above-mentioned regime-inherent norm translations, just one topic strongly reflects a norm that is extraneous to the UNFCCC regime. As part of the topic of 'land use and anti-capitalism', a small group of NDCs proposes alternative norms based on anti-capitalism. 15 Venezuela and Bolivia stand out here, as they agree that the capitalist system destroys Mother Earth and the climate system. As translations of anti-capitalism, Venezuela proposes 'eco-socialism' (Venezuela's NDC, p. 6, 9), and Bolivia refers to 'Living Well', which includes complementary rights for people's development and for Mother Earth (Bolivia's NDC, p. 2-3). This may also be the reason why both countries propose the eradication of carbon markets, thereby contesting one particular norm translation of cost-effectiveness (Venezuela's NDC, p. 39, Bolivia's NDC, p. 6). In sum, states mostly translate regimeinherent norms, while extraneous norm translations are rare and specific to few countries in the NDCs.

The words in this topic refer to particular vulnerabilities to climate change based on sea level rise and extreme weather events, such as heavy rainfall, flooding, cyclones, and drought. While this topic only sparsely contains actions or plans of adaptation to address those climate impacts, it includes (natural and societal) risks assessments and sets the priorities for more concrete adaptation interventions in the NDCs of the most representative cases. Illustrating another priority related to water, 51 states use the 'water scarcity management' topic to address specific interventions responding to vulnerabilities in the water supply, including both the needs of the general population and irrigation for farming. Less prominently, adaptation in the health sector due to the increase of diseases appears here as well. Focusing on a different and even narrower set of circumstances, 21 states translate the adaptation norm in the form of 'pastoralism and land management'. The most representative cases refer to the need for adaptation in the areas of sustainable land management (SLM), animal husbandry, and agroecology.

Two further topics indicate distinct ways of translating the regime-inherent norm of specific needs of developing countries. The 'international support' topic is emphasized by 54 states. They demand financial support, GCF (Green Climate Fund) projects, capacity building, or technology transfer to cover either the costs of climate actions and/or to meet the climate-related needs of developing countries. In contrast, the 'post-war rural measures' topic is only addressed by five countries. They refer to a very narrow version of specific needs related to special post-war circumstances in rural developing countries.

The regime-inherent norm of economic development is translated in the 'economic growth' topic. 72 states emphasize issues like the need for economic growth to alleviate poverty and to reduce unemployment of their growing populations. This can include shifts to more sustainable development pathways (e.g. Papua New Guinea) or the reliance on conventional fossil-fuel based development trajectories (e.g. Pakistan).

In sum, states have translated six of the eight regime-inherent norms of the Convention into their NDCs. This includes all the three norms explicitly highlighted in the 2014 Lima guidelines and three more that could broadly be perceived to fall under the categories of national and special circumstances. This indicates the strong normative path shaping role of those regime-inherent norms for the entirety of NDCs. Several of these norms have been translated in distinct and contrasting ways, such as renewable versus conventional energy or absolute versus relative emission reduction targets. We did not find references to either precautionary actions or free trade. 14 Those two regime-inherent norms reflected the mindset at the time of the Convention's drafting. While scientific uncertainty was reduced drastically with the periodical IPCC reports, free trade restrictions as carbon border adjustment measures were not a matter of discussion around 2015.

In contrast to the above-mentioned regime-inherent norm translations, just one topic strongly reflects a norm that is extraneous to the UNFCCC regime. As part of the topic of 'land use and anti-capitalism', a small group of NDCs proposes alternative norms based on anti-capitalism. 15 Venezuela and Bolivia stand out here, as they agree that the capitalist system destroys Mother Earth and the climate system. As translations of anti-capitalism, Venezuela proposes 'eco-socialism' (Venezuela's NDC, p. 6, 9), and Bolivia refers to 'Living Well', which includes complementary rights for people's development and for Mother Earth (Bolivia's NDC, p. 2-3). This may also be the reason why both countries propose the eradication of carbon markets, thereby contesting one particular norm translation of cost-effectiveness (Venezuela's NDC, p. 39, Bolivia's NDC, p. 6). In sum, states mostly translate regimeinherent norms, while extraneous norm translations are rare and specific to few countries in the NDCs.
sections.preview_head: States have translated the regime-inherent norm of mitigation responsibilities in four different ways, captured by four distinct topics on absolute emission targets, relative emission targets, UNFCCC collaboration for global mitigation efforts, and concrete domestic climate governance actions. paragraphs: Having discussed the range of individual norm translations inherent in the various topics, the sub-question remains: Which domestic context factors (approximated by Annex I status and membership in negotiation groups) can be linked to particular norm translations? The distinction between developed countries (Annex I) and developing countries (Non-Annex I) reflects not only different historical positions on climate change, but also different material domestic factors, such as levels of emissions, income, and vulnerability to climate change. Our analysis shows that this divide continues to shape norm translations, confirming previous findings of strong differences between their NDCs (Jernnäs and Linnér 2019; Mills-Novoa and Liverman 2019; Pauw et al. 2019;Leinaweaver and Thomson 2021). Additionally, we turn to the more specific country-level characteristics represented by membership in negotiating groups. This provides more nuanced hints on how prominently norm translations vary in line with domestic context factors. We focus on instances when groups of states prioritize aspects that stand out from their broader peer group (Annex I or Non-Annex I).

Annex I countries prioritize a narrow range of topics and, thus, regime-inherent norms (see Fig. 4). On average, they use more than half of their NDCs to refer to the topic of 'emission pledges and markets' that translates the norms of mitigation responsibilities and cost-effectiveness. Annex I countries thus follow the pattern of declaring absolute GHG emission reduction targets and the usage of carbon markets, which has been their typical approach to taking the lead in limiting climate change since the Kyoto Protocol. This indicates the role of these countries' historical positions and their high levels of GHG emissions and income. For similar reasons, Annex I countries prioritize 'UNFCCC collaboration', reflecting the ambition and fairness of their NDCs as a translation of mitigation responsibilities in order to defend their contribution to the global mitigation efforts. By contrast, they devote hardly any space to topics translating adaptation or specific needs. These omissions are in line with their domestic context factors of high income and low vulnerability to climate change. Overall, developed countries thus prioritize mitigation, which reflects their traditional approach in the UNFCCC and reluctance to ponder on additional commitments, such as on the provision of international support to developing countries. Unsurprisingly, the negotiation groups comprising Annex I states (EU, Umbrella, EIG), mostly follow the same patterns (see Fig. 5). Yet, we also find a few deviations from these prioritizations that point to the importance of particular historical positions for norm translations. The Umbrella states prioritize 'conventional energy' as translation of mitigation sectors. This indicates that these states still struggle to shift towards renewable energy, which is in line with their traditionally more mitigation reluctant historical position. The EIG countries, meanwhile, prioritize 'economic growth' as translation of economic development. Yet, its members articulate a green growth discourse (based on economic growth and reducing emissions) in line with their traditionally more ambitious historical position.

Non-Annex I countries address a wider range of norms in their NDCs (Fig. 4). Compared to Annex I countries, they talk much less and in a very different way about mitigation. Non-Annex I countries translate mitigation responsibilities in the form of concrete 'domestic governance' aspects and 'relative emission pledges'. The latter are based on rising BAU emissions or GDP, and are often partly conditional on international support, while Annex I countries committed to absolute emission reduction targets compared to a historical base year (see also Pauw et al. 2019;Tobin et al. 2018). Moreover, Non-Annex I states devote more space to concrete actions in different economic mitigation sectors. Overall, they place more emphasis on mitigation than previous research suggested (cf., Leinaweaver and Thomson 2021;Mills-Novoa and Liverman 2019). This stance is in line with their historical position (often rejecting absolute emission cuts but emphasizing domestic efforts) and their (mostly) low to medium levels of GHG emissions and income. In line with these domestic factors, Non-Annex I countries, moreover, emphasize specific needs (international support) and growth of economic development much more than Annex I countries. 16Broadly speaking, these patterns are consistent among all Non-Annex I groups, but we find some particularities in line with domestic characteristics. Some states place greater emphasis than others on traditional growth trajectories for economic development and 'conventional energy' as translation of mitigation sectors. This involves groups of middle to high income countries with medium to high emissions (BASIC, LMDC, OPEC) that share historical positions (rejection of mitigation commitments and prioritization of high economic growth) and of which some have economies dependent on fossil-fuel production (OPEC).

Zooming in on one important group shows further particularities. Among the Non-Annex I countries, BASIC most strongly emphasizes 'UNFCCC collaboration' and 'emissions pledges and markets' as translations of mitigation responsibilities. This indicates that middle income countries with relatively high emissions feel the expectation to justify the ambition and fairness of their mitigation contributions. Some, like Brazil, have begun to pledge absolute emission reductions. Yet, BASIC also prioritizes the request of 'international support' as a translation of specific needs. BASIC, thus, seems to put its own mitigation efforts much more in the context of international support requirements than other developing countries. This is best understood from their historical position that demanded international support for developing countries' climate actions. However, it may potentially lead to competition about the limited climate funding between richer and poorer developing countries.

Zooming out on all Non-Annex I countries again, we find that they prioritize the translation of adaptation, especially in the form of 'adaptation governance' and additionally by sector-specific interventions. This is in line with their overall higher vulnerability to climate change than Annex I states. Two particularly vulnerable groups most strongly prioritize the norm of adaptation in general and put their strongest focus on 'adaptation governance'. This involves island states with high vulnerability to sea-level rise (AOSIS) and least developed countries, which are particularly impacted in the agricultural sector (LDC). Based on the specific type of vulnerability, they additionally highlight 'water-related disasters' (AOSIS) or 'pastoralism and land management' (LDC) as different translations of adaptation.

Moreover, 'renewable energy' features more strongly than 'conventional energy' as translations of mitigation sectors in Non-Annex I's NDCs, indicating promises of an energy transformation in the Global South. The strongest prioritization of renewables can be found in states that combine high vulnerability and a particular ambitious historical position. The vulnerable island states of AOSIS have historically shared an ambitious historical position, demanding ambitious climate actions from all countries. In their NDCs, they therefore engage in mitigation for survival by prioritizing 'renewable energy' the most as a translation of mitigation sectors. Compared to traditionally mitigation-reluctant Non-Annex I states with medium to high levels of income and emissions (BASIC, OPEC, LMDC), who prioritize 'conventional energy' over 'renewable energy', these vulnerable developing countries engage in leapfrogging.foot_11 This is remarkable, considering the lower levels of economic resources of some of their members, and shows their ambition to contribute to global climate mitigation and act as role models for the shift to renewable energy.

Finally, solely some Non-Annex I states prioritize extraneous norms. 'Land use and anti-capitalism' as the single topic involving extraneous norm translation illustrates the importance of domestic cultural backgrounds. Here, two groups of Non-Annex I countries prioritize very different aspects: The explicitly anti-capitalist and traditionally confrontational ALBA states stand out by bringing forward anticapitalist norms. By contrast, AILAC members also prioritize this topic but strictly focus on aspects regarding mitigation actions related to land-use as translation of mitigation sectors. That is in line with their ambitious and constructive stance, evident also in their leading position among Non-Annex I states in terms of general prioritization of mitigation responsibilities and mitigation sectors.

Looking at patterns that cut across the Annex I divide, we find a few instances in which norm translations vary in line with similar domestic context factors. Firstly, (absolute) 'emission pledges and markets' as translations of mitigation responsibilities and cost-effectiveness can be attributed to middle to high income countries with high emissions (EU, Umbrella, EIG, BASIC), indicating the catching up of some emerging economies to industrialized countries in terms of mitigation pledges. Yet, this only includes states whose economies are not dependent on fossil-fuel production (in distinction to OPEC). Secondly, prioritizations of conventional energy as translation of mitigation sectors can be ascribed to middle to high income countries with medium to high emissions and mitigation-reluctant historical positions (Umbrella, OPEC, BASIC, LMDC). This shows the difficulties of these countries in transforming their energy systems for the benefits of limiting climate change.

To sum up, based on our explorative analysis, we find that particular norm translations vary in line with specific (combinations of) domestic context factors. Both material factors, such as levels of emissions, income or vulnerability as well as cultural factors, such as anti-capitalist beliefs, can be linked to varying norm prioritizations. Some outcomes additionally reflect historical positions within the UNFCCC, for example regarding states' level of ambition. Together, these factors shape states' implementation pledges under the Paris Agreement.
