paragraphs: Our analysis leads us to five main insights, which we elaborate in the following before ending with prospects for future research. First, our approach allowed us to identify eight regime-inherent norms, most of which were not only embedded in the 1992 Convention but also addressed in follow-up documents like the 2014 Lima Guidelines on NDC preparation. The empirical analysis then revealed that the 15 identified topics in the NDCs translate six out of eight regime-inherent norms (all except precautionary actions and free trade). This indicates the benefits in conceptualizing the international regime as containing a norm bundle that includes several norms with distinct normative expectations (cf., Fukuda-Parr and Hulme 2011; Bloomfield and Scott 2017).

Second, our approach allowed us to remain inclusive towards normative alternatives articulated by states. Yet, we only found one instance of states translating the regime-extraneous norm of anti-capitalism. 18 This suggests that perspectives proposing a system change play only marginal roles (cf., Jernnäs and Linnér 2019;Mills-Novoa and Liverman 2019). Contestation in the form of open articulation of alternatives to UNFCCC's norms is rare and specific to few countries. Instead, the UNFCCC's norm bundle appears to have considerable staying power, evident as states still mostly embrace the same regime-inherent norms that all parties agreed upon in 1992. This can be explained by the broad normative coverage of the norm bundle, as every state ensured its normative perspective to be included in the 1992 Convention, allowing them to continuously invoke the norms they had always preferred when engaging with the regime. Consequently, the more bottom-up NDC formulation process did not result in more references to extraneous norms, as one might have expected from a norm contestation perspective (cf., Wiener 2018). Instead, liberal environmentalism and ecological modernization continue to be embedded in in the NDCs, as evident in norm translations based on carbon markets and economic growth (cf., Jernnäs and Linnér 2019, see also Bernstein 2002;Bäckstrand and Lövbrand 2006). Thus, it seems unlikely that these discourses will face fundamental contestation in the future, indicating the stability of the liberal order in the climate regime.

Third, we could show that some norms were translated in several distinct and contrasting ways, such as absolute versus relative emission reduction targets as translations of mitigation responsibilities or renewable versus conventional energy as distinct translations of mitigation sectors (in distinction to Jernnäs and Linnér 2019;Mills-Novoa and Liverman 2019;Pauw et al. 2018;Pauw et al. 2020). These norm translations are shared by several states and indicate how they plan to implement larger discourses like 'ecological modernization' (in distinction to Jernnäs and Linnér 2019). In theoretical terms, this shows the benefits of our approach, which 18 Of course, some states might have mentioned country-specific regime extraneous norms in individual NDCs without us noticing: words only mentioned in one document are excluded from the quantitative analysis. Still, our results indicate that no other regime-extraneous norms stood out in the overall text corpus as a collectively shared norm translation by several states. takes the middle ground between norm researchers focusing on full-fledged adoption (such as in Finnemore 1996 or Risse et al. 1999) and those emphasizing the novelty of each individual translation (i.e., prior norm translation approaches): Norms can be translated in distinct ways, but, at the same time, can be shared by more than one actor.

Fourth, we theorized that norm translation depends on domestic context factors and operationalized this by membership in Annex I and in more specific negotiation groups. Overall, we find hints that the norm translation results indeed vary in line with (the combinations of) country-level specifics. We identified domestic factors that appear to have shaped states' norm translations, such as levels of vulnerability to climate change, GHG emissions and income as well as historical UNFCCC positions and cultural backgrounds. Annex I states prioritize mitigation norms and cost-effectiveness, whereas Non-Annex I states additionally emphasize aspects of adaptation, economic development and specific needs. This mirrors their differences in income, emissions, vulnerability, and historical positions. We also found more nuanced variation, for example that vulnerable countries with ambitious historical positions prefer renewable energy (AOSIS) while many countries with medium to high levels of income and emissions and a traditional mitigation-reluctant historical position still opt for conventional energy (Umbrella, OPEC, BASIC, LMDC). 19 Our analysis supports arguments in the norm literature that norm translations are shaped by domestic context. While others found cultural factors (cf., Berger 2017; Zimmermann 2017), we also revealed material factors shaping norm translations.

Fifth, topic modeling paired with qualitative validation of representative documents is a useful method for open-ended, exploratory analyses of how norms are being translated and what priorities different actors set. This method allowed us to reveal patterns in the NDCs based on their empirical prominence rather than prior expectations. Our results support many of the previous qualitative findings on NDCs, showing that the topic model has picked up coherent patterns in the text corpus. Moreover, compared to category-based qualitative content analyses, we were also able to reveal new insights. Our approach can be applied to much larger text corpora and used to trace changes over time, thus answering the call for 'additional methods that can offer in-depth analysis to large-n investigations' (Tobin et al. 2018: 18). Yet, topic modeling has limits when it comes to identifying subtle differences in how individual norms are being translated. Researchers interested in the nuances of (largely shared) norm translations should complement exploratory quantitative analysis with qualitative case studies.

Notwithstanding the specific character of the climate regime, including the bottom-up process of NDC formulation, we argue that this kind of analysis will also be applicable to norm bundles of other international regimes that give states the 19 Our findings suggest that the Lima Guidelines were not a major influence in this regard. While Non-Annex I countries emphasize all norms mentioned in the Lima Guidelines, individual groups did not follow its particular requests: LDCs and AOSIS did not overly prioritize mitigation sectors over mitigation responsibilities, and emerging economies like BASIC did not overly prioritize mitigation responsibilities over mitigation sectors. opportunity to articulate their plans for implementation. We expect that research on other norm bundles, for instance on trade or human rights, would also find different regime-inherent and regime-extraneous norm translations in line with countryspecific characteristics. Yet, the balance between regime-inherent and extraneous norms and the exact roles played by the different domestic context factors is subject to future research.

Several important avenues remain for future research on the international climate regime. So far, the norm bundle has proved quite stable over time, and it seems unlikely that alternative perspectives will prevail. As a case in point, the introduction of new carbon market instruments in the Paris Agreement and their subsequent operationalization follows the long-standing norm of cost-effectiveness. Yet some aspects that have historically been underemphasized, like adaptation, now play a stronger role in states' perspectives -particularly in the Global South (Kuyper et al. 2018). As the intensity of the climate crisis grows, a hierarchy of norms may even emerge in the future. Upcoming discussions of carbon border adjustments in the Global North, which seem to go against the Convention's original notion of protecting free trade, provide first indications in that direction. This underlines the importance of analyzing the relationships (and potential conflicts) between the UNFCCC's regime-inherent norms (cf. Harris and Symons 2013). Moreover, scholarly attention should be devoted to changes in the salience of norms over time. These aspects could be explored further by expanding the analysis to domestic policy documents that give scholars the opportunity to study normative change and prioritizations over time. After all, the normative perspectives expressed by states lay out humanity's paths on how to deal with the challenge of climate change.
